games.explanation: Pittsburgh keeps getting priced like the home team matters more than the rosters do, and tonight that math finally cracks. Keller is fine against righties and a problem against lefties, which is unfortunate for him because Cincinnati runs out a lineup that hits him from both sides and stacks the kind of contact quality he can't suppress. Elly De La Cruz in particular has been a different hitter against right-handers this year, slugging into a .339 xwOBA that Keller's profile does nothing to slow down. Singer walks too many to be elite but he's the better arm in this game, and the Reds are the better team by a meaningful gap. Getting plus money on the side the simulation likes outright is the entire pitch.

Misiorowski's strikeout numbers look like an ace's until you notice the walks underneath, and a rookie who hands out free passes at this rate is exactly the wrong arm to lay this kind of price with. The Nationals run out a stack of right-handed bats who actually fare better than the lefties against him, and his walk rate north of 11% means Washington doesn't need to square him up to manufacture innings. Irvin is the obvious hole in the boat, but the sim still lands inside a run because Milwaukee's lineup isn't the murderer's row the price implies. At +135 on a game projecting as a coin flip, you're getting paid to bet against a command profile that hasn't been tested at this altitude of expectations.

Wheeler is the better pitcher and nobody is arguing otherwise, but a plus price on the road dog assumes Miami can't scratch out four runs against him, and that's where the market is sleepwalking. Stowers has been mashing right-handed pitching all year with a .395 xwOBA and the kind of barrel rate that doesn't care about Wheeler's reputation, and Marsee and Lopez round out a top of the order that puts the ball in play and makes pitchers work. Eury Perez isn't Wheeler, but he misses enough bats to keep Schwarber and Harper from turning this into a track meet, and the closed roof neutralizes the one thing Philadelphia's lineup does better than anyone. The simulated final lands inside a run, which is exactly the spot where +105 on the underdog prints money over time.

Emmet Sheehan is being treated like a finished product when he's still a rookie with command issues against lefties and a fastball that gets squared up when hitters don't chase. The Cardinals run a lineup that doesn't strike out much at the top, and Iván Herrera in particular has been demolishing right-handed pitching this year with a barrel rate that travels in any park. Liberatore isn't going to win a Cy Young debate with anyone, but Dodger Stadium in 63 degrees with the homer factor playing down is the friendliest version of his start he's going to get. At +155 you're getting a live underdog priced like a tomato can, and the gap between what the board sees and what's actually on the field is the whole reason to be here.

Getting plus money on the Angels feels wrong until you notice the Mets aren't actually the heavyweight the line implies, and the matchup tilts a lot closer to a coin flip than the board wants to admit. Trout and Neto at the top of this order are the kind of right-handed thump that travels in any park, and the wind blowing in turns Soto from a solo-shot threat into a long out. The sim has this thing inside a run either way, which is exactly the script you want when you're getting paid to take the dog. At +109, the market is charging you nothing to be on the side with the better top of the order.

Cleveland is being treated like the home favorite out of habit, but the lineup behind Cantillo doesn't actually scare anyone outside of Ramirez, and Cantillo himself has a real problem with left-handed bats. That's a problem when the Athletics can run Soderstrom and Kurtz at him, the latter posting a .423 xwOBA against righties this year that plays directly into Cantillo's worst splits. Ginn isn't an ace but he's a perfectly serviceable counter in a game the market has priced as a clear Guardians edge when it's closer to a coin flip. At -110 on the team our numbers make a 59% favorite, you're getting plus-money value on the side that should be chalk.
